20.27: 'There came to him some Sadducees, those who deny that there is a resurrection, and they asked him a question' — the Sadducees who 'deny that there is a resurrection' are the priestly-aristocratic party who accepted only the Torah (not the Prophets or Writings) as authoritative, and who found no resurrection doctrine in the Five Books. Their arrival after the Pharisees' tribute-trap is the second confrontation of a different kind: not political entrapment but theological challenge from a different direction. Christ will answer both the political trap and the theological challenge from the same source: the nature of God revealed in Scripture.
20.28: 'Saying, Teacher, Moses wrote for us that if a man's brother dies, having a wife but no children, the man must take the wife and raise up offspring for his brother' — the levirate law (Deut 25:5-6 — the brother-in-law's duty to marry the widow and preserve the family name) is the Sadducees' setup for their resurrection reductio ad absurdum. The law is real and serious (it preserves the widow's social security and the family's heritage). But the Sadducees use it to construct an absurd scenario (seven brothers, one wife) designed to make resurrection seem logically impossible. They argue from the law that resurrection undermines the law — Christ will answer that their conception of resurrection is wrong, not that the law is wrong.
20.29: 'Now there were seven brothers. The first took a wife, and died without children' — the seven-brothers scenario is constructed to maximize the absurdity: the maximum number of levirate obligations, all unfulfilled by death (no children), producing a situation where the resurrection would require assigning one woman to seven simultaneous husbands. The Sadducees assume that resurrection-life is exactly like this-age life with its social structures, legal arrangements, and marriage institutions. Their error is the error of assuming that the age to come is a mere continuation of this age — they have not grasped the discontinuity between the present order and the resurrection order.
20.30: 'And the second' — the second brother takes the widow, and likewise dies childless. The second iteration of the levirate chain deepens the absurdity the Sadducees are constructing. The serial levirate marriage scenario, whatever its historical plausibility (perhaps a reference to Tobit, where the demon killed Sarah's seven husbands), serves to pile up the logical difficulty: by the seventh iteration, the situation has become so convoluted that resurrection seems impossible to adjudicate. Christ will cut through the whole scenario by correcting the premise rather than solving the puzzle within the premise's framework.
20.31: 'And the third took her, and likewise all seven left no children and died' — all seven brothers married the same woman; all seven died childless. The scenario is complete: the levirate chain has run its full course without producing offspring, leaving the widow with seven sequential husbands all of whom predecease her. The Sadducees have constructed the maximum possible complication for the resurrection-claim. The complication rests on their assumption that resurrection-life maintains the marriage structures of this-age life. Christ will address this assumption directly in v.35-36, showing that the premise of the question is incorrect.
20.32: 'Afterward the woman also died' — the woman's death completes the cast: eight deaths (seven brothers plus the woman), no surviving children, maximum levirate complexity, maximum resurrection-puzzle. Now the question can be posed. The Sadducees have done their setup work carefully. But they have built on a false premise — the assumption that the resurrection is a resumption of present-age social arrangements — and Christ will demolish the premise before addressing the puzzle. The woman's death, too, is included in the scenario because the question 'whose wife will she be?' (v.33) requires her also to be among the resurrected.
20.33: 'In the resurrection, therefore, whose wife will the woman be? For the seven had her as wife' — the question is posed with studied care: 'whose wife will she be in the resurrection?' assumes that the resurrection restores the same social relationships that existed before death, including all their legal complications. It is a genuine question only if the resurrection is a return to the present social order. Christ's answer will reveal that the resurrection is not a return to the present order but a transformation into a new order — and that the question itself reveals the Sadducees' misunderstanding of what resurrection is.
20.34: 'And Jesus said to them, The sons of this age marry and are given in marriage' — the phrase 'the sons of this age' (hoi huioi tou aiōnos toutou) distinguishes the present age from the age to come. Marriage belongs to the present age — it is the institution God established for human flourishing and covenant-propagation in this age (Gen 2:24; Matt 19:4-6). The Sadducees have correctly observed that marriage belongs to this age; their error is assuming it belongs to the age to come in the same way. Christ affirms the institution's this-age validity while correcting its extension into the resurrection-order. He is not abolishing marriage but situating it properly within salvation-history.
20.35: 'But those who are considered worthy to attain to that age and to the resurrection from the dead neither marry nor are given in marriage' — 'considered worthy' (kataxiōthentes — those deemed worthy to attain) is not merit-language but eschatological status-language: those who attain the resurrection-age are those who are in Christ (1 Cor 15:22 — 'in Christ shall all be made alive'). They 'neither marry nor are given in marriage' — not because marriage is sinful or inferior but because the age to come has a different social order that does not require the institutions of this age (propagation, covenant-securing through family, protection of the widow through levirate). The resurrection order is qualitatively different, not merely quantitatively extended.
20.36: 'For they cannot die anymore, because they are equal to angels and are sons of God, being sons of the resurrection' — the deathlessness of resurrection-life is the root of the marriage-institution's transformation: marriage as an institution serves this age partly as propagation (if no one died, propagation would eventually be unnecessary). In the age to come, 'they cannot die anymore' — the institution's propagation-purpose has been accomplished; the family of God is complete. 'Equal to angels' (isangeloi — a hapax in the NT) specifies the deathlessness, not the asexuality or non-embodiment of resurrection; the Sadducees who denied angels (Acts 23:8) are now confronted with angelomorphic resurrection-life as the answer to their question.
20.37: 'But that the dead are raised, even Moses showed, in the passage about the bush, where he calls the Lord the God of Abraham and the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob' — Christ's answer to the Sadducees uses their own authority (Moses, whose Torah they exclusively accepted) to prove resurrection. The burning bush passage (Exod 3:6) is the proof-text: God declares 'I am the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob' — present tense, 'I am,' not 'I was.' If God's present relationship with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob is real, those patriarchs must exist. The present-tense 'I am' of God's covenant-declaration requires the aliveness of the covenant partners. Moses proves resurrection to the Sadducees from their own canon.
20.38: 'Now he is not God of the dead, but of the living, for all live to him' — the theological principle behind the burning-bush proof: God cannot be 'God of' those who simply do not exist. If God is the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, then those three men are alive — their bodies await resurrection, but their persons are alive to God. 'All live to him' (pantes gar autō zōsin) is the eschatological affirmation: from God's perspective, all who are in covenant with him live — death is not the end of the covenant relationship but a threshold within it. Christ who is himself 'the resurrection and the life' (John 11:25) grounds this in his own person: in him, all live.
20.39: 'Then some of the scribes answered, Teacher, you have spoken well' — the scribes' commendation of Christ's answer to the Sadducees is from people who shared the Pharisaic belief in resurrection (Acts 23:8 — the Pharisees acknowledged both resurrection and angels). They recognize Christ's answer as correct within their own theological framework — the present-tense God-of requires living covenant-partners. The commendation is honest and ironic: the scribes who are among those planning Christ's death (19:47) affirm the truth of his teaching about resurrection. They affirm what he teaches while planning to kill the one in whom the teaching is embodied.
20.40: 'For they no longer dared to ask him any question' — the silence of all opponents after the resurrection-question marks the end of the Passion-week controversy dialogues. The tribute-question, the authority-question, and the resurrection-question have all been answered with wisdom that leaves the questioners silenced. The authorities' silence is not the silence of assent but the silence of strategic withdrawal — they will move from public questioning to private conspiracy (22:3-6). When argument fails, they resort to betrayal. Christ's victory in the controversy dialogues will be reversed (humanly speaking) by the Judas-betrayal that their silence leads them to arrange.
20.41: 'But he said to them, How can they say that the Christ is David's son?' — having silenced his opponents, Christ now takes the initiative with his own question: the riddle of the Christ's identity as simultaneously David's son and David's Lord. The question 'how can they say that the Christ is David's son?' is not a denial of Davidic descent (Luke has established it in the genealogy, 3:31; and in the angel's words, 1:32) but a challenge to those whose entire messianic expectation is exhausted by Davidic-sonship. If the Christ is only David's son (a political king in David's dynasty), Psalm 110 is inexplicable. There must be something more.
20.42: 'For David himself says in the Book of Psalms, The Lord said to my Lord, Sit at my right hand' — Ps 110:1 (ho Kyrios tō kyriō mou — 'the LORD said to my Lord') is the most quoted OT text in the NT, appearing in Acts 2:34-35; 1 Cor 15:25; Heb 1:13; 10:13. David's 'my Lord' (the second Kyrios) is a superior — David's lord, to whom YHWH speaks. David addressing the Christ as 'my Lord' establishes the Christ's supra-Davidic identity: he is the son of David by lineage but the Lord of David by nature. The genealogical relationship is real but insufficient to describe what the Christ is. The Davidic sonship is a category that contains but does not exhaust his identity.
20.43: 'Until I make your enemies your footstool' — the enthronement of the Christ at YHWH's right hand 'until his enemies become his footstool' is the Ascension-to-Parousia description in miniature. Christ is at the Father's right hand now (Acts 2:33-35 — Peter's Pentecost sermon citing this exact verse); his enemies are being subjected to him through the gospel now; his enemies will be finally and completely subjected at the Parousia (1 Cor 15:25 — 'he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet'). The 'until' is the present age; the footstool is the final subjugation of all that opposes the kingdom.
20.44: 'David thus calls him Lord, so how is he his son?' — the unanswerable question leaves the crowd (and the reader) with the puzzle that only the Incarnation resolves: the Christ is both David's son (in the flesh) and David's Lord (by nature). Rom 1:3-4 gives the fullest NT answer: 'descended from David according to the flesh and declared to be the Son of God in power according to the Spirit of holiness by his resurrection from the dead.' The Incarnation is the 'how' of the Psalm's seeming paradox: the Lord of David became the son of David without ceasing to be the Lord. The riddle's answer is the person of Jesus Christ.
20.45: 'And in the hearing of all the people he said to his disciples' — the shift from public controversy to semi-private teaching (directed to disciples, heard by all) is Luke's characteristic literary structure: Jesus teaches the disciples while the crowd overhears. The teaching that follows (about the scribes' behavior) is addressed to those who will need to distinguish authentic from performative religion in the coming age of the church. The disciples who have been watching the scribes question Christ must also be warned against adopting the scribes' patterns — the enemy without is also a temptation within.
20.46: 'Beware of the scribes, who like to walk around in long robes, and love greetings in the marketplaces and the best seats in the synagogues and the places of honor at feasts' — the scribes' performance of status (long robes = visible dignity; marketplace greetings = public honor; best synagogue seats = positional authority; honor at feasts = social priority) is the anti-pattern of the kingdom. The long robe that marks status is the opposite of the servant's apron (12:37 — the master puts on the servant's apron). The best seat is the opposite of the last place (14:10). The honor-at-feast is the opposite of the host who invites the poor (14:13). Christ's own life is the inversion of every scribal status-marker.
20.47: 'Who devour widows' houses and for a pretense make long prayers. They will receive the greater condemnation' — the indictment of the scribes moves from public performance to private exploitation: 'devouring widows' houses' is the abuse of legal guardianship over vulnerable women for financial gain. The long prayers are the religious cover for the exploitation — the performative piety that conceals practical predation. The 'greater condemnation' (perissoteron krima — a more severe judgment) falls on those who exploit the vulnerable under the cover of religious devotion: the higher the religious pretension, the greater the accountability. The widow who will give everything in the next verse (21:2) is the implicit contrast to the scribes who take everything from widows.
